content.problemStatement:
It's the sale season again and Chef bought items worth a total of XXX rupees. The sale season offer is as follows:


if X≤100X \le 100X≤100, no discount.
if 100<X≤1000100 \lt X \le 1000100<X≤1000, discount is 252525 rupees.
if 1000<X≤50001000 \lt X \le 50001000<X≤5000, discount is 100100100 rupees.
if X>5000X \gt 5000X>5000, discount is 500500500 rupees.
Find the final amount Chef needs to pay for his shopping.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single line of input containing an integer XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the final amount Chef needs to pay for his shopping.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the total amount (in rupees) of Chef's purchase.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the final amount Chef has to pay after applying the discount.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For X=15 and X=70, X<=100 so no discount. For X=250, 100<X<=1000 so discount=25, final=225. For X=1000, same range, discount=25, final=975.
X=100 is exactly the boundary of the first range, so no discount.

solution.approach:
For each test case we read X and determine the discount using a chain of if‑else conditions:
- If X <= 100, discount = 0.
- Else if X <= 1000, discount = 25.
- Else if X <= 5000, discount = 100.
- Else discount = 500.
The answer is simply X - discount. This runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
